s: The bomb-proof bunker under Chancery Lane that survived the Blitz
w: Forty-plus shops of silver in a bunker under Chancery Lane, many family businesses for three generations. During the Blitz a direct hit destroyed the building above; the vaults — walls 1.2 metres of steel-lined concrete — survived intact. World's largest collection of silver for public sale.
_facts: Established in the 1800s as a safe deposit company renting strong rooms for household silver and documents
During WWII a bomb struck the building above, but the vaults with 1.2-metre steel-lined walls survived undamaged
Since 1953 the vaults have operated as an underground marketplace with over 40 shops
Many dealer families have occupied their shops for 50+ years across multiple generations
Described as home to the world's largest collection of silver for sale to the public

s: Whitechapel's forgotten Ashkenazi burial ground — opened 1761, closed by 1857
w: One of London's oldest Ashkenazi Jewish burial grounds, laid out in Whitechapel in 1761 and closed to burials less than a century later. The community had moved east. Headstones lean in the long grass, Hebrew letters worn smooth. A quiet acre that time and the city forgot together.
_facts: Opened 1761 as the burial ground for the New Synagogue
Also served the Great Synagogue community
Closed to further burials in 1857
Located on Brady Street in Whitechapel, London Borough of Tower Hamlets
Contains several notable graves of the Ashkenazi Jewish community

s: The Barbican's dry run — built on City bombsite rubble by the same architects
w: Chamberlin, Powell and Bon built this estate on City of London bombsite rubble in the 1950s before turning to the Barbican next door. Great Arthur House was Britain's tallest residential block when completed. Grade II listed since 1997 — a proof of concept for everything that followed.
_facts: Built in the 1950s on a site devastated by WWII bombing on the northern edge of the City of London
Designed by Chamberlin, Powell and Bon, the same architects responsible for the Barbican
Great Arthur House at its centre was the tallest residential building in Britain when constructed
Grade II listed since 1997 as a site of special architectural interest
Preceded and informed the design approach for the adjacent Barbican development

s: Free botanical garden on a roof, under the UK's largest timber structure
w: Free public garden on the roof of the Elizabeth line's Canary Wharf station, sheltered by the largest timber structure in the UK — a lattice canopy by Foster + Partners. Plants from east and west hemispheres face each other across a meridian line. Winner of the 2017 European Garden Award.
_facts: Located on the roof of Crossrail Place, which houses Canary Wharf Elizabeth line station
Opened 1 May 2015
The roof is described as the largest timber project in the United Kingdom
Designed by Foster + Partners and Arup
Plants are organised from eastern and western hemispheres divided by a meridian line
Won first prize for Innovative Design of a Contemporary Garden at 2017 European Garden Awards in Berlin

s: Rosalind Franklin is buried here. So is Nathan Rothschild.
w: Rosalind Franklin is buried here, alongside Nathan Rothschild, 1st Baron. Opened 1873, the cemetery holds 29,800 graves and a Grade II listing on Historic England's parks register — once nicknamed the 'Rolls-Royce' of London's Jewish cemeteries. Known to few outside the community.
_facts: Established in 1873 in Beaconsfield Road, Willesden
Contains 29,800 graves
Grade II status on Historic England's Register of Historic Parks and Gardens
Rosalind Franklin, who made key contributions to discovering DNA's molecular structure, is buried here
Nathan Rothschild, 1st Baron Rothschild, is buried here
Has been described as the 'Rolls-Royce' of London's Jewish cemeteries

s: Ancient woodland recorded in the Domesday Book, managed by the City of London
w: Ancient woodland between Highgate, Muswell Hill and East Finchley — part of the old Forest of Middlesex, recorded in the Domesday Book. Twenty-eight hectares managed by the City of London Corporation, miles from the Square Mile. A fragment older than the city that surrounds it.
_facts: 28-hectare area of ancient woodland in North London
Part of the ancient Forest of Middlesex, which spanned London, Hertfordshire and Essex
Mentioned in the Domesday Book, confirming its ancient status
Managed by the City of London Corporation despite being in the London Borough of Haringey
Located between East Finchley, Highgate and Muswell Hill

s: The pub that named Fitzrovia — Dylan Thomas, Orwell, Augustus John, all regulars
w: The pub that named a neighbourhood. Judah Kleinfeld hung out a sign reading 'Fitzroy Tavern' in 1919 and the streets around it became Fitzrovia. Through the 1920s to 50s the bar packed in Dylan Thomas, George Orwell, Augustus John and Nina Hamnett. Samuel Smith's now. The spirit remains.
_facts: Originally built as the Fitzroy Coffee House in 1883, converted to a pub in 1887
Judah Morris Kleinfeld renamed it 'Fitzroy Tavern' in March 1919
The surrounding area became known as Fitzrovia, named after the pub (which was named after the Fitzroy family, Dukes of Grafton)
Famous as a bohemian gathering place during the 1920s to mid-1950s
Notable regular patrons included Dylan Thomas, George Orwell, Augustus John, Jacob Epstein and Nina Hamnett
Now owned by Samuel Smith Old Brewery

s: Lubetkin's Clerkenwell block — restored in 2008 to its Constructivist colours
w: Lubetkin's most complete post-war realisation of his 1930s radical vision, restored in 2008 to its original palette — Russian Constructivist reds and blues recalled from his Moscow contacts. Grade II* listed, squeezed between Rosebery Avenue and St John Street in Clerkenwell. Living proof.
_facts: Designed by Berthold Lubetkin, situated between Rosebery Avenue and St John Street in Clerkenwell EC1
Described as 'the most complete post-war realisation of a 1930s radical plan'
Grade II* listed
2008 restoration brought back the original colour scheme recalling Russian Constructivism
Now a mixed community of private owners and council tenants, run by a resident-elected management organisation

s: Lenin lived here. Lubetkin buried a bronze memorial to him under the staircase.
w: Lenin edited Iskra here in 1902, exiled in a Finsbury flat. Lubetkin planned to call the new block 'Lenin Court' after the war; the Cold War renamed it Bevin Court instead. His tribute — a bronze memorial — now lies buried under the constructivist staircase at the heart of it. Grade II*.
_facts: Built 1954 by Lubetkin (Tecton practice), completed with Francis Skinner and Douglas Carr Bailey
Occupies the site of Lenin's 1902-03 London home, where he edited the socialist newspaper Iskra
Originally planned as 'Lenin Court', renamed Bevin Court after anti-communist Foreign Secretary Ernest Bevin
A Lenin memorial created by Lubetkin now lies buried under the central constructivist staircase
Grade II* listed since December 1998
Restored by London Borough of Islington 2014-2016

s: The Walks open since 1597 — and Shakespeare's Comedy of Errors first played here
w: The Walks have been open since at least 1597. Shakespeare's Comedy of Errors is believed to have had its first performance in the hall just behind. Francis Bacon lived and worked at Gray's Inn, shaping the garden. Still closed on weekday lunchtimes, briefly open otherwise. Blink and miss it.
_facts: Gray's Inn Gardens (the Walks) have existed since at least 1597
Gray's Inn Hall is believed to be where Shakespeare's Comedy of Errors was first performed
Francis Bacon was a prominent member and treasurer of Gray's Inn and influenced the garden design
One of the four Inns of Court, located at the junction of High Holborn and Gray's Inn Road
Gardens are accessible to the public but with restricted hours

s: London's only natural swimming lake open to the public — and once a golf course
w: The only natural open-water swimming lake open to the public in London — a Victorian boating lake restored as a wild swimming spot in 2019, after decades as a golf course. A Palladian mansion watches from the hill above. Ninety-six hectares of Lewisham that feel nothing like it.
_facts: Located in Beckenham Place Park, London Borough of Lewisham
Described as the only natural open-water swimming lake available to the public in London
The lake was restored and opened for swimming in 2019
The park previously housed a golf course until 2016
Contains a Palladian-style mansion now serving as a community centre and cafe
The park covers 96 hectares, the largest green space in Lewisham

s: A 1693 merchant's house where harpsichords are sometimes played for visitors
w: Built by a merchant in 1693 and sitting in its walled Hampstead garden ever since, Fenton House holds the Benton Fletcher collection of early keyboard instruments — harpsichords and spinets sometimes played for visitors during opening hours. Grade I listed. One acre. National Trust.
_facts: Completed in 1693, date inscribed on a chimney breast
Grade I listed building
Houses the Benton Fletcher collection of early keyboard instruments, including harpsichords and spinets
The instruments are sometimes played for visitors during opening hours
Bequeathed to the National Trust in 1952 by Lady Binning, its last resident
Features a large walled garden (1 acre) with sunken garden, orchard with ~30 apple varieties, and kitchen garden

s: Monks died here for refusing Henry VIII. Brothers still live here today.
w: Founded on a 1348 Black Death burial ground, it became a Carthusian priory in 1371. At dissolution in 1537, Prior Houghton was hanged, drawn and quartered at Tyburn. Elizabeth I used the buildings for coronation preparations. An almshouse since 1611 — Brothers still live here today. Tours most days.
_facts: Founded on a Black Death burial ground from 1348
Established as a Carthusian priory in 1371 by Walter de Manny
Dissolved in 1537; Prior John Houghton and ten monks were executed for refusing to affirm Henry VIII's religious authority
Elizabeth I used the buildings in 1558 during preparations for her coronation
Thomas Sutton purchased and established an almshouse here in 1611; over 40 Brothers (residents) still live here
Museum and guided tours available since 2017; site survived Blitz damage

s: Holden's 1932 brick drum — one of twelve Great Modern Buildings per The Guardian
w: Charles Holden designed this 1932 Piccadilly line station after a Scandinavian study tour — the circular brick drum of the ticket hall borrowed from Swedish modernism. Grade II* listed since 2011. The Guardian named it one of twelve Great Modern Buildings. A masterpiece in the ordinary commuter belt.
_facts: Opened 19 September 1932 on the Piccadilly line extension from Finsbury Park
Designed by architect Charles Holden after a study tour of Scandinavia
Features a distinctive circular drum-like ticket hall of brick and glass with a flat concrete roof
Influenced by Swedish modernism, with some sources crediting Gunnar Asplund's work
Grade II listed in 1971, upgraded to Grade II* in July 2011
Named one of twelve 'Great Modern Buildings' by The Guardian, described as 'a total and entire work of art'

s: The highest point on the London Underground above ground — 60 feet over a stream
w: The highest above-ground point on the whole London Underground — a Northern line viaduct nearly 60 feet above Dollis Brook in West Finchley, carrying trains of the old Edgware, Highgate and London Railway. Walk beneath it on the loop path; the Tube carriages overhead seem remarkably small.
_facts: Railway viaduct to the west of Finchley, North London, carrying the Northern line between Mill Hill East and Finchley Central
Holds the record as the highest above-ground point on the London Underground at nearly 60 feet (18 m)
Originally part of the Edgware, Highgate and London Railway
Also known as Dollis Road Viaduct, Dollis Viaduct or Mill Hill Viaduct
Located at approximately 51.61 N, 0.20 W in North London
